content.question: Do selection policies that admit higher-achieving low-SES students into 6/8-year gimnázium programs produce individual-level mobility gains sufficient to offset system-wide segregation and left-behind peer effects, or do aggregate distributional harms outweigh targeted mobility gains?
content.reduction_path: Decompose cohort-level outcome distributions comparing gimnázium-admitted low-SES students (tracking their adulthood outcomes) against non-admitted peers and against counterfactual scenarios of universal non-selective secondary enrollment, using microsimulation or causal-decomposition methods to isolate selection-induced gains from peer-effect harms.
